subject: ap-government
title: Concept Application — Senate Filibuster and Election Reform
stimulus:
In the summer of 2021, the House of Representatives passed a bill on election reform with overwhelming support from Democrats and no Republican votes. When the same legislation was discussed in the Senate later on in the year, the Democrats had a majority to pass the bill but could not obtain enough Republican votes to end the debate. As long as debate continued, there could be no final vote on the legislation. As a result, the bill did not become law, despite having the minimum number of supporters to win a floor vote if one were to be held.

Disappointed by the outcome, some of the bill's supporters suggested that the problem was not the legislation but the rules of the Senate. Groups pressured the Senate leadership to consider revising the rules to make it easier for the chamber to pass legislation, similar to the rules currently in place in the House of Representatives.

Opinions on changing the Senate rules varied. Some Republican and Democratic senators supported the rule change. Others in both parties opposed it, voicing concerns about the long-term consequences of changing the rules. One senator noted that because any party could be in the minority in the future, any changes to the rules should still ensure that all senators continue to have input.

parts.prompt: Describe the Senate procedure at the center of the controversy in the scenario.
parts.scoring_notes: Accept 'filibuster,' 'cloture rule,' or description of the supermajority requirement to end debate. The response must describe the procedure, not just name it.

scoring_points.description: Describe the Senate procedure at the center of the controversy in the scenario.

required_elements: 1 pt: Describes the filibuster (or related cloture rules). Accept: during debate, a senator can use the filibuster to prolong debate and delay or prevent a vote on a bill; the Senate requires a supermajority (60 votes) to bring debate to an end before a final vote; the cloture rule requires at least sixty senators to vote to end debate before a bill is brought to the floor for a final vote.
alternatives.correct_example: The filibuster is a Senate procedure that allows senators to extend debate indefinitely, preventing the chamber from moving to a final vote. To end a filibuster, the Senate requires 60 votes to invoke cloture — a supermajority threshold that is much higher than the simple majority needed to pass the bill itself.

scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for describing the Senate procedure at the center of the controversy in the scenario. Acceptable descriptions: during debate, a senator can use the filibuster to prolong the debate and delay or even prevent a vote on a bill; the Senate requires a supermajority to bring debate to an end before a vote can be held on a bill; the cloture rule requires that at least sixty senators vote to end debate, which must happen before a bill is brought to the floor for a final vote.

sample_responses.response: The filibuster is a Senate procedure that allows a senator to prolong debate and delay or even prevent a final vote on a bill.
sample_responses.commentary: Accurately describes the filibuster — the procedure at the center of the scenario where debate cannot be ended. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: The cloture rule requires that at least sixty senators vote to end debate before a bill can be brought to the floor for a final vote.
sample_responses.commentary: Accurately describes cloture as the supermajority threshold needed to end debate — this is the rule the Democrats could not reach in the scenario. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Senators vote on bills by majority rule.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not describe the specific procedure (filibuster/cloture) at the center of the scenario. 0 pts.

parts.prompt: Explain how the procedure described in part A makes passing legislation more difficult in the Senate compared with the House of Representatives.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must compare the Senate's filibuster/cloture rules to the House's simpler majority process, explaining why the Senate rules make passage harder.

scoring_points.description: Explain how the procedure described in part A makes passing legislation more difficult in the Senate compared with the House of Representatives.

required_elements: 1 pt: Explains the Senate-House comparison. Accept: passing legislation is harder in the Senate because senators can use the filibuster to delay or prevent a vote whereas the House has limited debate time; passing legislation in the Senate requires a supermajority (60 votes) to invoke cloture, while the House lacks a cloture rule and only requires a simple majority; strict rules regarding time and relevance in the House limit obstruction, while those rules do not apply to Senate debate.
alternatives.correct_example: 1 pt: Explains the Senate-House comparison. Accept: passing legislation is harder in the Senate because senators can use the filibuster to delay or prevent a vote whereas the House has limited debate time; passing legislation in the Senate requires a supermajority (60 votes) to invoke cloture, while the House lacks a cloture rule and only requires a simple majority; strict rules regarding time and relevance in the House limit obstruction, while those rules do not apply to Senate debate.

scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for explaining how the procedure described in part A makes passing legislation more difficult in the Senate compared with the House of Representatives. Acceptable explanations: passing legislation is harder in the Senate because senators can use the filibuster to delay or even prevent a vote on a bill whereas the other chamber has limited debate time that moves the bill through faster; passing legislation in the Senate is harder than in the House because in the Senate it requires a supermajority to invoke cloture while the House lacks a cloture rule and only requires a simple majority to break debate; there are strict rules regarding time and relevance in the House which limit the ability of members to obstruct the passage of legislation while those same rules do not apply to debate in the Senate.

sample_responses.response: Passing legislation is harder in the Senate because senators can use the filibuster to delay or even prevent a vote on a bill, whereas the House has strict time limits on debate that move a bill through faster.
sample_responses.commentary: Directly compares the Senate (filibuster enables delay) to the House (limited debate time) to explain why passing legislation is harder in the Senate. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Passing legislation in the Senate is harder because it requires a supermajority of 60 votes to invoke cloture. The House lacks a cloture rule and only needs a simple majority to end debate, so bills move through much more easily.
sample_responses.commentary: Uses the supermajority-vs-simple-majority comparison to explain Senate-House differences. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: The Senate has more senators than the House has representatives, so it is harder to get enough support.
sample_responses.commentary: Inaccurate — the Senate has 100 members while the House has 435. Does not explain the procedural difference (filibuster/cloture vs. simple majority). 0 pts.

parts.prompt: Explain how the senators' actions in addressing the election reform bill illustrate the concept of partisanship.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must explain partisanship as voting/acting along party lines — and connect that to the specific scenario of Republicans blocking cloture despite Democrats' majority.

scoring_points.description: Explain how the senators' actions in addressing the election reform bill illustrate the concept of partisanship.

required_elements: 1 pt: Explains how the senators' actions illustrate partisanship. Accept: while Democrats had a simple majority, strict voting along party lines meant they did not have enough votes to invoke cloture and bring the bill to a final vote; Republicans practiced partisan voting when they voted against ending the filibuster — by voting together as a bloc, they prevented the Democrats from achieving their policy goals despite holding a minority of seats.
alternatives.correct_example: 1 pt: Explains how the senators' actions illustrate partisanship. Accept: while Democrats had a simple majority, strict voting along party lines meant they did not have enough votes to invoke cloture and bring the bill to a final vote; Republicans practiced partisan voting when they voted against ending the filibuster — by voting together as a bloc, they prevented the Democrats from achieving their policy goals despite holding a minority of seats.

scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for explaining how the senators' actions in addressing the election reform bill illustrate the concept of partisanship. Acceptable explanations: while the Democrats had a simple majority of the seats in the Senate, strict voting along party lines meant they did not have enough votes to bring about cloture and bring the bill to the floor for a vote; Republicans practiced partisan voting when they voted against ending the filibuster in this scenario — by voting together, they were able to prevent the Democrats from achieving their policy goals even though the Republicans had a minority number of seats in the Senate.

sample_responses.response: The Republicans practiced partisan voting when they united as a bloc to vote against ending the filibuster. By voting together along party lines, they were able to prevent the Democrats from reaching the 60-vote cloture threshold, which illustrates partisanship because senators' votes were driven by party loyalty rather than by the merits of the bill.
sample_responses.commentary: Clearly illustrates partisanship by explaining how party-line voting allowed a minority party to block legislation in the scenario. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Although the Democrats had a majority of seats, they could not achieve cloture because strict voting along party lines meant they did not have the 60 votes needed — this illustrates partisanship because senators voted with their parties instead of across party lines.
sample_responses.commentary: Ties the scenario outcome directly to party-line voting as an illustration of partisanship. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Partisanship is when people belong to political parties.
sample_responses.commentary: Defines partisanship generically without connecting it to the senators' actions in the scenario. 0 pts.
